text: A transverse incision is made in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament. The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space and creates a preperitoneal flap, continuing dissection towards the symphysis pubis and Cooper's ligament.

A transverse incision is made in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament. The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space and creates a preperitoneal flap, dissecting down towards the symphysis pubis and Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon reduces the preperitoneal fat incarcerated into the right inguinal and femoral hernia. Attention is then directed towards the right incarcerated obturator hernia, where dissection is performed to reduce the preperitoneal fat from the obturator perineal defect.

The surgeon reduces the preperitoneal fat incarcerated into the right inguinal and femoral hernia. Attention is then directed towards the right incarcerated obturator hernia, where dissection is performed to reduce the preperitoneal fat from the obturator perineal defect.

The surgeon carries out dissection medially towards the symphysis pubis, then inserts a three-dimensional mesh into the preperitoneal space. Dissections are performed laterally, superiorly, and inferiorly to create adequate space for the large mesh placement.

The surgeon carries out dissection medially towards the symphysis pubis. A three-dimensional mesh is inserted into the preperitoneal space, and dissection continues laterally, superiorly, and inferiorly to create adequate space for the large mesh placement.

The surgeon makes a transverse incision on the left side, entering the preperitoneal space. Dissection is performed laterally, superiorly, inferiorly, and medially towards the symphysis pubis to prepare the area for mesh placement.

A transverse incision is made on the left side, and the surgeon enters the preperitoneal space. Dissection is performed in multiple directions: laterally, superiorly, inferiorly, and medially towards the symphysis pubis.
The surgeon reduces the preperitoneal fat incarcerated in the left obturator hernia. A large three-dimensional mesh is then inserted into the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon reduces the preperitoneal fat incarcerated in the left obturator hernia. Subsequently, a large three-dimensional mesh is inserted into the preperitoneal space.

The surgeon lays the mesh into the preperitoneal space to cover all hernia defects. The mesh is secured to the abdominal wall using T-seal. The peritoneum is then closed with a two-hole D-lock absorbable suture in a running fashion.
